title: API stability policy
text: PESTO follows published two-phase policy. phase boundary 1.0.0 release.

title: API stability policy
text: Versions 0.x.y follow additive--intent evolution. Every minor release (0.x → 0.(x+1)) adds new exports without breaking existing signatures; patch releases fix bugs refresh documentation. Track record far: 0.1.0 — first release (core IES / GLM solvers, SVD backends, surrogates, PEST++ integration, visualisation). 0.1.1 — sole-authorship consolidation; API change. 0.2.0 — added pesto_ies_callback() (-process IES driver) apsim_callback() (apsimx adapter). breaking changes. 0.3.0 — added pesto_ensemble_manifest (S7 cross-package contract) plus as_manifest(), write_manifest(), read_manifest(), verify_manifest(). breaking changes. 0.3.1 — added format = c("rds","","csv") write_manifest(); backwards-compatible YAML. 0.3.2 — renamed format = "csv" "csv_unverified" every call site; legacy "csv" still accepted deprecation warning, 0.3.1 manifests round-trip cleanly. 0.3.3 — Makevars FLIBS portability fix; API change. policy “additive--intent” rather “frozen”: pre-1.0 reserves right break existing signature design flaw surfaces, every change must : Listed ## Breaking changes heading NEWS.md, first. Justified release notes. feasible, accompanied temporary back-compat shim (v0.3.2 csv → csv_unverified rename template: legacy spelling still works deprecation warning). depend PESTO pre-1.0, pin version renv.lock DESCRIPTION (Imports: PESTO (>= 0.3.3)).

title: API stability policy
text: 1.0.0, PESTO adopts strict frozen-API additive evolution — policy glmnet, mgcv, long-lived solver-style packages: Signatures exported functions never change backwards-incompatible way across major versions. New capability arrives via new entry points (new exports), never via changes existing ones. existing function genuinely needs retired, marked lifecycle::deprecate_warn(), kept ≥ 2 minor versions, promoted lifecycle::deprecate_stop() ≥ 1 minor version, removed next major release. Successors signposted deprecation message. policy chosen PESTO invoked IES / surrogate backend pipeline code maintainer edit — kernR consumes pesto_ensemble_manifest S7 contract; APSIM ensemble loops via pesto_ies_callback() written run life calibration campaign. Silent breakage across versions defeat contract.

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: pesto_ies() shells pestpp-ies binary, writes reads .pst / ensemble / observation files every realisation. -process R forward model – apsimx wrapper APSIM, Python bridge, fast synthetic test problem – file round-trip pure overhead. pesto_ies_callback() keeps C++ ensemble kernel (ensemble_solution(), Chen & Oliver 2013) drives outer loop R, calling forward model -process. APSIM difference re-reading .apsimx file every realisation holding model memory across whole ensemble. document shows: driver synthetic linear-Gaussian problem (recovers truth). typed forward-model contract (pesto_forward_model()) shared adapter modes, including parallel, fault-tolerant evaluation. shape apsim_callback() adapter apsimx (run block disabled – needs APSIM installed). Multi-fidelity calibration: cheap surrogate ramped expensive model fidelity_schedule.

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: Run six IES iterations single lambda: Diagnostics: Posterior RMSE small fraction prior RMSE, mean phi decrease across iterations. full per-realisation phi trajectory:
code: library(PESTO)  npar  <- 4L nobs  <- 8L nreal <- 120L sigma <- 0.05  G          <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * npar), nobs, npar) theta_true <- c(1.0, -0.5, 2.0, 0.25) y          <- as.numeric(G %*% theta_true) + rnorm(nobs, sd = sigma) names(y)   <- paste0("o", seq_len(nobs))  # Forward model: any function that takes an (nreal x npar) matrix and # returns an (nreal x nobs) matrix. forward <- function(theta) theta %*% t(G)  prior <- matrix(rnorm(nreal * npar), nreal, npar,                 dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("p", seq_len(npar)))) fit <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward_model  = forward,   prior_ensemble = prior,   obs            = y,   obs_sd         = sigma,   noptmax        = 6L,   lambda         = 1.0,   verbose        = FALSE ) prior_rmse <- sqrt(mean((colMeans(prior) - theta_true)^2)) post_mean  <- colMeans(as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, -1L])) post_rmse  <- sqrt(mean((post_mean - theta_true)^2))  cat(sprintf("prior RMSE: %.4f\n", prior_rmse)) #> prior RMSE: 1.1300 cat(sprintf("posterior RMSE: %.4f\n", post_rmse)) #> posterior RMSE: 0.0232  mean_phi <- vapply(fit$iterations, `[[`, numeric(1L), "mean_phi") print(mean_phi) #> [1] 17452.123014     1.534500     1.533925     1.533362     1.532812 #> [6]     1.532273 plot_phi(fit) #> Warning in melt.data.table(phi_dt, id.vars = iter_col, measure.vars = phi_cols, #> : 'measure.vars' [realisation, phi] are not all of the same type. By order of #> hierarchy, the molten data value column will be of type 'double'. All measure #> variables not of type 'double' will be coerced too. Check DETAILS in #> ?melt.data.table for more on coercion.

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: bare function(theta) -> obs quickest way , carries metadata: driver know output dimension ahead time, run realisations parallel, express failure budget. pesto_forward_model() wraps callable typed contract . single object native-callback driver classic .pst path (pesto_ies()) built honour, forward model travels modes unchanged.
code: fm <- pesto_forward_model(   fn          = forward,   n_obs       = nobs,   param_names = paste0("p", seq_len(npar)),   on_failure  = "na" )  # A contract object is evaluated directly with pesto_evaluate(); the # returned matrix carries the per-realisation failure count. sim <- pesto_evaluate(fm, prior) cat(sprintf("evaluated %d x %d; failures: %d\n",             nrow(sim), ncol(sim), attr(sim, "n_failures"))) #> evaluated 120 x 8; failures: 0  # Passing the contract object to the driver is equivalent to passing the # bare function -- the bare form is auto-wrapped internally. fit_typed <- pesto_ies_callback(fm, prior, y, sigma,                                 noptmax = 6L, verbose = FALSE) identical(   as.matrix(fit_typed$par_ensemble[, -1L]),   as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, -1L]) ) #> [1] TRUE

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: expensive forward model – APSIM ensemble especially – realisations within one iteration embarrassingly parallel. Build contract parallel = "multicore" evaluation engine dispatches rows across forked workers via parallel::mclapply(). reproducible draws, set "L'Ecuyer-CMRG" RNG first; realisation receives independent stream. custom map_fn (lapply-shaped function) escape hatch cross-platform cluster backends, example future.apply::future_lapply mirai map. Failures governed on_failure ("na" tolerates, "stop" aborts) max_fail_frac, aborts fraction failed realisations single evaluation exceeds budget – guardrail silently collapsing ensemble.
code: RNGkind("L'Ecuyer-CMRG") set.seed(42L)  fm_par <- pesto_forward_model(   fn       = forward,   n_obs    = nobs,   parallel = "multicore",   n_cores  = 4L ) fit_par <- pesto_ies_callback(fm_par, prior, y, sigma, noptmax = 6L)

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: single consequential number hand pesto_ies_callback() obs_sd – observation standard deviation, sets IES weights w=1/σobsw = 1/\sigma_{\mathrm{obs}}. also easiest get wrong, way fails silently. failure mode -determination: conditioning ensemble likelihood far tighter data deserve. smoother drives every realisation onto point, posterior spread collapses, run reports answer credible interval far narrow honest – confidently wrong. usual route trap statistical, typo. Suppose target observation mean mm field replicates. replicate spread – genuine measurement-plus-process noise single modelled value must match – σ\sigma. standard error mean σ/m\sigma/\sqrt{m}, smaller factor m\sqrt{m} shrinks average plots. Passing standard error obs_sd tells smoother data pin model roughly m\sqrt{m} times sharply really . forward model right, ensemble nowhere go onto single collapsed point. contrast runs synthetic problem twice. first call passes field-realistic replicate spread sigma; second passes standard error mean m = 40 replicates, eight times small. collapse announce posterior mean. linear-Gaussian toy runs recover truth three figures. announces posterior spread whether credible interval still covers truth: -precise run’s posterior spread small fraction honest run’s, 90 per-cent credible band stopped covering truth entirely. ensemble confident wrong. PESTO records collapse directly. ensemble_spread_ess() – spectral participation ratio parameter anomaly covariance, effective number variance-carrying directions – logged every iteration spread_ess ratio spread_ess_ratio (relative ensemble size). ratio falls steeply toward zero across iterations diagnostic signature ensemble draining spread: fix set obs_sd uncertainty modelled value must actually reproduce – replicate-level measurement-plus-process spread, never standard error average: Use replicate standard deviation σ\sigma, σ/m\sigma/\sqrt{m}, target mean mm measurements. Fold structural model-discrepancy obs_sd – forward model approximation, likelihood pretend matches reality tightly model can. genuine doubt, err wide. -wide obs_sd leaves spread table merely -uses data; -narrow one destroys ensemble reports false confidence. -determination unavoidable – many observations, realisations – covariance inflation localisation countermeasures (vignette("inflation-localisation")) actively replenish collapsing spread rather diagnosing . honest obs_sd first line defence, countermeasures second.
code: m      <- 40L obs_se <- sigma / sqrt(m)   # the standard error of a 40-replicate mean  fit_ok <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward, prior, y,   obs_sd  = sigma,          # field-realistic replicate spread   noptmax = 6L, verbose = FALSE )  fit_collapsed <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward, prior, y,   obs_sd  = obs_se,         # the over-precise likelihood -- the trap   noptmax = 6L, verbose = FALSE ) spread <- function(fit) {   mean(apply(as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, -1L]), 2L, sd)) }  coverage_90 <- function(fit, truth) {   pe <- as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, -1L])   lo <- apply(pe, 2L, quantile, 0.05)   hi <- apply(pe, 2L, quantile, 0.95)   mean(truth >= lo & truth <= hi)   # fraction of params inside the band }  data.frame(   obs_sd          = c(sigma, obs_se),   case            = c("field-realistic", "over-precise (SE of mean)"),   mean_post_sd    = c(spread(fit_ok), spread(fit_collapsed)),   coverage_90_pct = 100 * c(coverage_90(fit_ok, theta_true),                             coverage_90(fit_collapsed, theta_true)) ) #>        obs_sd                      case mean_post_sd coverage_90_pct #> 1 0.050000000           field-realistic 3.560357e-03              50 #> 2 0.007905694 over-precise (SE of mean) 9.266982e-05               0 ess_ratio <- function(fit) {   vapply(fit$iterations, `[[`, numeric(1L), "spread_ess_ratio") }  rbind(   `field-realistic` = round(ess_ratio(fit_ok), 3),   `over-precise`    = round(ess_ratio(fit_collapsed), 3) ) #>                  [,1]  [,2]  [,3]  [,4]  [,5]  [,6] #> field-realistic 0.341 0.342 0.342 0.343 0.344 0.345 #> over-precise    0.340 0.340 0.340 0.340 0.340 0.340

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: adapter wraps apsimx realisation gets working copy APSIM template, parameter edits per param_map, run, extraction step. signature returned matches pesto_ies_callback() expects.
code: library(apsimx)   # >= 2.7.0 from CRAN # Requires a working APSIM Next Gen installation.  forward <- apsim_callback(   template  = "wheat_wagga.apsimx",   param_map = list(     RUE = "Wheat.Leaf.Photosynthesis.RUE.FixedValue",     CN2 = "Soil.SoilWater.CN2Bare"   ),   output_extractor = function(sim) {     # sim is the data.frame of report-table variables.     as.numeric(sim$Wheat.Grain.Total.Wt)   } )  prior <- cbind(   RUE = runif(40, 1.0, 2.0),   CN2 = runif(40, 60,  90) )  fit <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward_model  = forward,   prior_ensemble = prior,   obs            = c(y_2018 = 4500, y_2019 = 5200, y_2020 = 4900),   obs_sd         = 250,   noptmax        = 4L )  summary(fit$par_ensemble)

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: Per-realisation APSIM crashes (corrupt config, solver divergence, missing report variable) caught adapter emerge NA rows. pesto_ies_callback()’s on_failure = "na" (default) carries realisations forward unchanged ensemble survives partial failures. Setting on_failure = "stop" aborts soon one row missing.

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: apsim_callback() closure writes realisation uniquely-named working file, safe evaluate parallel: wrap pesto_forward_model(parallel = "multicore") exactly ensemble runs across forked workers, invoking APSIM input. Start modest n_cores – apsimx’s thread-safety heavy ensemble load independently characterised. pure-R synthetic models serial loop already fast enough overhead dominated ensemble_solution() .

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: Process-based crop models expose cost/accuracy dial: daily time-step lite soil profile cheap; sub-daily step full profile expensive. pesto_multifidelity_model() makes dial first-class. bundles ordered stack fidelity levels – cheapest first – IES driver picks level per iteration fidelity_schedule, early iterations explore cheaply late iterations sharpen expensive truth. final ensemble always refreshed highest fidelity. cheap level run whole ensemble expensive level subset, mf_control_variate() lifts cheap outputs toward expensive ones variance-minimising affine correction – plug-primitive surrogate cascade:
code: # Cheap level: the linear model with a small systematic bias. # Expensive level: the unbiased truth. cheap_fn     <- function(theta) theta %*% t(G) + 0.30 expensive_fn <- forward  mf <- pesto_multifidelity_model(   levels = list(     pesto_forward_model(fn = cheap_fn,     n_obs = nobs, fidelity = 0L),     pesto_forward_model(fn = expensive_fn, n_obs = nobs, fidelity = 1L)   ),   costs = c(1, 25)   # the expensive level is ~25x dearer per run )  # Ramp: two cheap iterations, then four expensive ones. fit_mf <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward_model     = mf,   prior_ensemble    = prior,   obs               = y,   obs_sd            = sigma,   noptmax           = 6L,   fidelity_schedule = c(0L, 0L, 1L, 1L, 1L, 1L),   verbose           = FALSE )  mf_rmse <- sqrt(mean(   (colMeans(as.matrix(fit_mf$par_ensemble[, -1L])) - theta_true)^2 )) cat(sprintf("multi-fidelity posterior RMSE: %.4f\n", mf_rmse)) #> multi-fidelity posterior RMSE: 0.2592 sub      <- 1:20 low_all  <- pesto_evaluate(mf, prior, level = 0L) low_sub  <- low_all[sub, , drop = FALSE] high_sub <- pesto_evaluate(mf, prior[sub, , drop = FALSE], level = 1L)  corrected <- mf_control_variate(low_all, high_sub, low_sub) cat(sprintf("mean |cheap - corrected| over the ensemble: %.4f\n",             mean(abs(low_all - corrected)))) #> mean |cheap - corrected| over the ensemble: 0.3000

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: every forward model simulator behind executable. large class – crop growth, epidemic dynamics, nutrient solute transport, pharmacokinetics – small system ordinary differential equations integrated forward time, observation vector read state trajectory. PESTO ships ready--use templates return typed pesto_forward_model() everything , plug straight IES driver, multi-fidelity stack, manifest emitter. Integration self-contained fixed-step RK4 default (extra dependency); solver = "desolve" path delegates deSolve package stiff systems. generic builder ode_forward_model(): supply derivative function function(t, y, theta) -> dydt, initial state, time grid, theta columns model consumes. Two specialisations come pre-built. crop_growth_forward_model() logistic dry-matter accumulation curve dB/dt = r B (1 - B / b_max) – canonical sigmoid description seasonal biomass, calibrating r, b_max, b0 observed biomass--time series. seir_forward_model() closed-population Susceptible-Exposed-Infectious-Recovered epidemic model, calibrating transmission, latency, recovery rates beta, sigma, gamma observed infectious-prevalence curve. reproduction number R0=β/γR_0 = \beta / \gamma recoverable summary outbreak curve identifies sharply: templates return pesto_forward_model(), cheap-coarse / fine-grid fidelity trick previous section applies verbatim: build one template coarse n_steps one fine n_steps, stack pesto_multifidelity_model(), ramp integration resolution across IES iterations.
code: times <- seq(0, 120, by = 15) crop  <- crop_growth_forward_model(times = times)  # Simulate a biomass series at a known parameter, then add field noise. theta_crop <- c(r = 0.06, b_max = 1400, b0 = 20) biomass    <- as.numeric(pesto_evaluate(   crop, matrix(theta_crop, nrow = 1L, dimnames = list(NULL, names(theta_crop))) )) y_crop        <- biomass + rnorm(length(biomass), sd = 20) names(y_crop) <- paste0("t", seq_along(y_crop))  # Invert: a broad prior over the three growth parameters. prior_crop <- cbind(   r     = runif(120L, 0.02, 0.12),   b_max = runif(120L, 900, 2000),   b0    = runif(120L, 5, 60) ) fit_crop <- pesto_ies_callback(crop, prior_crop, y_crop, obs_sd = 20,                                noptmax = 8L, verbose = FALSE) post_crop <- colMeans(as.matrix(fit_crop$par_ensemble[, -1L]))[names(theta_crop)] rbind(truth = theta_crop, posterior = round(post_crop, 3)) #>               r    b_max     b0 #> truth     0.060 1400.000 20.000 #> posterior 0.063 1371.492 18.251 days <- seq(0, 60, by = 5) seir <- seir_forward_model(times = days, n_pop = 1000, i0 = 1)  theta_seir <- c(beta = 0.6, sigma = 0.2, gamma = 0.1) prevalence <- as.numeric(pesto_evaluate(   seir, matrix(theta_seir, nrow = 1L, dimnames = list(NULL, names(theta_seir))) )) y_seir        <- prevalence + rnorm(length(prevalence), sd = 3) names(y_seir) <- paste0("d", seq_along(y_seir))  prior_seir <- cbind(   beta  = runif(200L, 0.3, 0.9),   sigma = runif(200L, 0.1, 0.4),   gamma = runif(200L, 0.05, 0.2) ) fit_seir  <- pesto_ies_callback(seir, prior_seir, y_seir, obs_sd = 3,                                 noptmax = 10L, verbose = FALSE) post_seir <- colMeans(as.matrix(fit_seir$par_ensemble[, -1L]))[names(theta_seir)] cat(sprintf("R0 truth = %.1f, R0 posterior = %.2f\n",             theta_seir[["beta"]] / theta_seir[["gamma"]],             post_seir[["beta"]] / post_seir[["gamma"]])) #> R0 truth = 6.0, R0 posterior = 5.54

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: pesto_ies_callback() smoother: assimilates every observation one batch. -season setting – growing season’s observations arrive time want updated parameter posterior season progresses, harvest – pesto_ies_filter() filter. assimilates time-ordered observation windows one another static calibration parameters; window’s posterior becomes next window’s prior, information accrues posterior tightens window window. posterior final window matches batch smoother recover, also intermediate posteriors – value filter trajectory, just endpoint. multi-fidelity stack plugs fidelity_schedule (one level per window: run cheap early, sharpen late), result wraps manifest contract via as_manifest() (tagged method = "ies_filter").
code: # Reuse the synthetic problem above; split the nobs observations into # three temporal windows (e.g. early / mid / late season). windows <- list(1:3, 4:6, 7:8)  fit_seq <- pesto_ies_filter(   forward_model  = forward,   prior_ensemble = prior,   obs            = y,   obs_sd         = sigma,   windows        = windows,   verbose        = FALSE )  # The per-window history carries the per-parameter ensemble sd; its mean # should fall window over window as more of the season is assimilated. sd_trace <- vapply(fit_seq$windows, function(w) mean(w$par_sd), numeric(1)) data.frame(   window      = seq_along(sd_trace),   obs_assim   = vapply(fit_seq$windows, function(w) length(w$obs_indices),                        integer(1)),   mean_par_sd = round(sd_trace, 4) ) #>   window obs_assim mean_par_sd #> 1      1         3      0.4007 #> 2      2         3      0.0180 #> 3      3         2      0.0152

title: Calibrating APSIM with PESTO (in-process callback)
text: Use classic .pst path : forward model lives behind non-R executable PEST++ can drive directly via template/instruction-file mechanism. need full bit--bit compatibility pestpp-ies behaviour (e.g. cross-validating existing PEST++ workflow). depend pestpp-ies-specific features yet exposed R driver (full lambda line-search, regularisation modes, Tikhonov priors). code: rec <- rbindlist(lapply(pnm, function(p) {   q <- stats::quantile(R$post[, p], c(.05, .5, .95), na.rm = TRUE)   data.table(Parameter = p, Truth = spec[[p]]$truth,              `Posterior mean` = round(mean(R$post[, p], na.rm = TRUE), 1),              `5%` = round(q[1], 1), `95%` = round(q[3], 1),              `Truth in 90% CI` = ifelse(spec[[p]]$truth >= q[1] &                                         spec[[p]]$truth <= q[3], "yes", "no")) })) knitr::kable(rec, caption = "Recovery of the known truth from noisy yields.") dens <- rbindlist(lapply(pnm, function(p) rbind(   data.table(Parameter = p, value = R$prior[, p],     dist = "prior"),   data.table(Parameter = p, value = R$post[, p],      dist = "posterior") ))) truth_dt <- data.table(Parameter = pnm,                        truth = vapply(spec, `[[`, 0, "truth")) ggplot(dens, aes(value, fill = dist)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.5, colour = NA) +   geom_vline(data = truth_dt, aes(xintercept = truth),              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.5) +   facet_wrap(~ Parameter, scales = "free") +   scale_fill_manual(values = c(prior = "grey70", posterior = PAL[1])) +   labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = NULL,        title = "Prior to posterior; dashed line = truth") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

what: Out-of-sample validation – and the under-dispersion caveat
title: Calibrating APSIM Wheat with PESTO: a case study
text: Using posterior ensemble predict held-seasons tests genuine --sample skill. honest finding raw ensemble’s predictive band narrow:  raw 90% band covers well 90% held-truth – finite-ensemble -dispersion iterative smoother suffers. PESTO ships remedy: covariance inflation (, spatial problems, localisation), documented Inflation localisation vignette. Re-running RTPS inflation widens posterior lifts coverage toward nominal. Inflation strength problem-dependent, report coverage tune rather trusting default blindly.
code: oe <- R$obs_ensemble band <- function(mat) {   data.table(year = R$season_year[R$val_idx],              truth = R$y_truth[R$val_idx],              lo = apply(mat[, R$val_idx, drop = FALSE], 2L, stats::quantile, .05, na.rm = TRUE),              hi = apply(mat[, R$val_idx, drop = FALSE], 2L, stats::quantile, .95, na.rm = TRUE)) } cov_of <- function(b) mean(b$truth >= b$lo & b$truth <= b$hi) b_base <- band(oe); b_base[, panel := sprintf("raw ensemble (%.0f%% covered)", 100 * cov_of(b_base))] panels <- list(b_base) if (!is.null(R$inflation)) {   b_inf <- band(R$inflation$obs_ensemble)   b_inf[, panel := sprintf("with RTPS inflation (%.0f%% covered)", 100 * cov_of(b_inf))]   panels <- list(b_base, b_inf) } bd <- rbindlist(panels) ggplot(bd, aes(year)) +   geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lo, ymax = hi), fill = PAL[1], alpha = 0.25) +   geom_point(aes(y = truth), colour = PAL[2], size = 1.3) +   facet_wrap(~ panel) +   labs(x = NULL, y = "Held-out yield (kg/ha)",        title = "Held-out seasons: 90% predictive band vs truth") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 10)

title: The Ensemble-Run Manifest: a Portable, Verifiable Record
text: PESTO ensemble run produces moderately large pile state: parameter ensemble, simulated outputs, target observations, IES weights, RNG seed, lambda schedule, runtime context. manifest captures versioned S7 object – pesto_ensemble_manifest – YAML + sidecar persistence SHA-256 integrity checking, run : reproducible auditable – complete, hash-verified record can re-open check later; portable – documented, versioned data contract downstream tool can read without reaching PESTO-specific list internals. deliberately infrastructure rather methodology: value every consumer reads object, provenance guarantees, every run.

what: Constructing a manifest from an IES run
title: The Ensemble-Run Manifest: a Portable, Verifiable Record
text: Wrap result: Slots reachable via standard S7 @ accessor:
code: library(PESTO)  set.seed(20260516) npar <- 3L; nobs <- 6L; nreal <- 60L  G <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * npar), nobs, npar) theta_true <- c(1.0, -0.5, 2.0) y <- as.numeric(G %*% theta_true) + rnorm(nobs, sd = 0.05) names(y) <- paste0("o", seq_len(nobs))  prior <- matrix(rnorm(nreal * npar), nreal, npar,                 dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("p", seq_len(npar))))  fit <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward_model  = function(theta) theta %*% t(G),   prior_ensemble = prior,   obs            = y,   obs_sd         = 0.05,   noptmax        = 4L,   verbose        = FALSE ) m <- as_manifest(fit, seed = 20260516L,                  apsim_version = NA_character_) print(m) #> <pesto_ensemble_manifest> schema 1.1.0 #>   run_id        : ies_callback_20260628_040559_fe6542df #>   method        : ies_callback  (noptmax=4) #>   ensemble      : 60 realisations x 3 parameters | 6 observations #>   failure rate  : 0.00% #>   pesto version : 0.8.0.9000  apsim: NA #>   timestamp     : 2026-06-28T04:05:59+0000 #>   data hash     : sha256:e7b630ad06429b528fa6a57a4973894eb9bf2709a6a3ffeb01366ede48b78ed6 m@run_id #> [1] "ies_callback_20260628_040559_fe6542df" m@data_hash #> [1] "sha256:e7b630ad06429b528fa6a57a4973894eb9bf2709a6a3ffeb01366ede48b78ed6" m@noptmax #> [1] 4 head(m@params) #>   real_name       p1         p2       p3 #> 1    real_1 1.029000 -0.4654747 2.011874 #> 2    real_2 1.026500 -0.4658594 2.013726 #> 3    real_3 1.032378 -0.4622419 2.014441 #> 4    real_4 1.031622 -0.4617477 2.015089 #> 5    real_5 1.034491 -0.4633193 2.014088 #> 6    real_6 1.034192 -0.4611789 2.017081

text: workflows need human-readable view ensemble – quick scans Excel, ad-hoc exports domain collaborator, copy-paste Quarto narrative – write_manifest() accepts format = "" argument emits CSV sidecars alongside RDS. hash stays bound RDS (bit-exact integrity preserved), YAML records inspection paths separate inspection_csv: block consumers can ignore : format = "csv_unverified" mode (renamed "csv" PESTO 0.3.2) available one-way exports non-R analysts round-trip integrity required. YAML carries integrity: not_verifiable downstream tools can branch weaker contract. verify_manifest() function returns ok = NA rather spurious FALSE CSV formatter precision loss (~1 ULP IEEE 754 epsilon) enough flip hash: need human inspection verifiable integrity, use format = "". csv_unverified mode deliberately named flag weaker contract every call-site. export, storage intend re-load trust.

code: out_rds <- file.path(dir, "wagga_2026_run01_outputs.rds") df <- readRDS(out_rds) df[1, 2] <- df[1, 2] + 1e-3       # perturb one cell saveRDS(df, out_rds, version = 3L)  m3 <- read_manifest(file.path(dir, "wagga_2026_run01.yaml")) v  <- verify_manifest(m3) v$ok #> [1] FALSE

title: Getting Started with PESTO
text: PESTO (Parameter ESTimation Optimised – PEST approach brought R) R package high-performance parameter estimation, uncertainty quantification, inverse modelling. brings algorithms PEST (Parameter ESTimation; Doherty 2015) C++ successor PEST++ (White et al. 2020) natively R ecosystem, extends typed forward-model contract, -process simulator callback, multi-fidelity acceleration, surrogate methods. title: Getting Started with PESTO
text: PESTO provides ensemble_solution() function – high-performance C++ implementation IES update equation. computational heart package:
code: set.seed(42) npar  <- 50 nobs  <- 100 nreal <- 30  # Generate synthetic ensemble differences par_diff  <- matrix(rnorm(npar * nreal, sd = 0.1), npar, nreal) obs_diff  <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * nreal, sd = 1.0), nobs, nreal) obs_resid <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * nreal, sd = 0.5), nobs, nreal) par_resid <- matrix(rnorm(npar * nreal, sd = 0.1), npar, nreal) weights   <- rep(1.0, nobs) parcov_inv <- rep(1.0, npar) Am <- matrix(rnorm(npar * (nreal - 1)), npar, nreal - 1)  # Run the ensemble update upgrade <- ensemble_solution(   par_diff    = par_diff,   obs_diff    = obs_diff,   obs_resid   = obs_resid,   par_resid   = par_resid,   weights     = weights,   parcov_inv  = parcov_inv,   Am          = Am,   cur_lam     = 1.0 )  cat("Upgrade matrix dimensions:", dim(upgrade), "\n") #> Upgrade matrix dimensions: 30 50 cat("Mean absolute upgrade:", mean(abs(upgrade)), "\n") #> Mean absolute upgrade: 0.01283321

rSVD dimensions: U =", dim(res_rsvd$u), ", V =", dim(res_rsvd$v), "

title: Countering Ensemble Collapse: Inflation and Localisation
text: iterative ensemble smoother estimates posterior parameter distribution finite ensemble realisations. finite ensemble two pathologies arise, make posterior spread narrow – ensemble becomes -confident: -dispersion (ensemble collapse). assimilation step contracts ensemble spread; several iterations posterior variance can fall far true posterior variance. smoother reports far certainty data support. Spurious correlations. finite ensemble manufactures apparent correlations parameters observations real. Acting injects noise update accelerates collapse. PESTO addresses first covariance inflation (pesto_inflation()) second covariance localisation (pesto_localisation()). opt-: default NULL leaves update identical bare smoother. vignette demonstrates effect linear-Gaussian problem, analytic posterior known collapse can measured exactly.

what: A linear-Gaussian problem with a known posterior
title: Countering Ensemble Collapse: Inflation and Localisation
text: linear forward model d=Gθ+εd = G\theta + \varepsilon Gaussian prior θ∼N(0,C0)\theta \sim N(0, C_0) observation error ε∼N(0,R)\varepsilon \sim N(0, R), posterior covariance Cpost=(C0−1+G⊤R−1G)−1C_{\mathrm{post}} = (C_0^{-1} + G^{\top} R^{-1} G)^{-1}. can therefore compare ensemble’s posterior spread directly truth. small helper runs smoother returns realised posterior spread alongside spread-ESS collapse diagnostic recorded final iteration.
code: library(PESTO) set.seed(42L)  npar  <- 6L nobs  <- 10L nreal <- 24L          # a deliberately small ensemble, to provoke collapse  G          <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * npar), nobs, npar) theta_true <- rnorm(npar) obs_sd     <- 0.3 y          <- as.numeric(G %*% theta_true) + rnorm(nobs, sd = obs_sd)  # Analytic posterior standard deviation (standard-normal prior). post_cov <- solve(diag(npar) + crossprod(G) / obs_sd^2) post_sd  <- sqrt(diag(post_cov))  forward <- function(theta) theta %*% t(G) prior   <- matrix(rnorm(nreal * npar), nreal, npar,                   dimnames = list(NULL, paste0("p", seq_len(npar)))) run_ies <- function(inflation = NULL, localisation = NULL) {   fit <- pesto_ies_callback(     forward_model  = forward,     prior_ensemble = prior,     obs            = setNames(y, paste0("o", seq_len(nobs))),     obs_sd         = obs_sd,     noptmax        = 12L,     inflation      = inflation,     localisation   = localisation,     verbose        = FALSE   )   par_post  <- as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, -1])   last_diag <- fit$iterations[[length(fit$iterations)]]   list(     sd_ratio  = mean(apply(par_post, 2L, sd) / post_sd),     ess_ratio = last_diag$spread_ess_ratio   ) }

title: Countering Ensemble Collapse: Inflation and Localisation
text: mean posterior standard deviation fraction analytic value – ensemble badly -confident. spread-ESS ratio quantifies collapse eigenspectrum parameter anomaly covariance (1 means variance spread isotropically across directions; small values mean collapsed onto ):
code: bare <- run_ies() round(bare$sd_ratio, 3) #> [1] 0.222 round(bare$ess_ratio, 3) #> [1] 0.315

title: Countering Ensemble Collapse: Inflation and Localisation
text: pesto_inflation() offers four methods. workhorse relaxation prior spread ("rtps", Whitaker & Hamill 2012): parameter’s posterior anomalies rescaled toward pre-update spread, directions collapsed hardest re-inflated . "adaptive" method instead targets global spread-retention floor. RTPS roughly doubles retained posterior spread lifts spread-ESS ratio. caveat worth stating plainly: inflation mitigates collapse, abolish . finite-ensemble GLM smoother size still -estimates posterior spread; inflation moves substantially closer truth without claiming reach . Larger ensembles narrow residual gap. spread-ESS diagnostic (ensemble_spread_ess()) recorded every iteration regardless method, collapse trajectory always available result:
code: rtps     <- run_ies(inflation = pesto_inflation("rtps", alpha = 0.6)) adaptive <- run_ies(inflation = pesto_inflation("adaptive",                                                 retention_floor = 0.7))  data.frame(   method        = c("none", "rtps", "adaptive"),   sd_ratio      = round(c(bare$sd_ratio, rtps$sd_ratio, adaptive$sd_ratio), 3),   spread_ess    = round(c(bare$ess_ratio, rtps$ess_ratio, adaptive$ess_ratio), 3) ) #>     method sd_ratio spread_ess #> 1     none    0.222      0.315 #> 2     rtps    0.507      0.436 #> 3 adaptive    0.310      0.459 fit <- pesto_ies_callback(   forward, prior, setNames(y, paste0("o", seq_len(nobs))),   obs_sd = obs_sd, noptmax = 12L,   inflation = pesto_inflation("rtps", alpha = 0.6), verbose = FALSE ) plot(   vapply(fit$iterations, function(d) d$spread_ess_ratio, numeric(1L)),   type = "b", pch = 19, xlab = "iteration", ylab = "spread-ESS ratio",   main = "Dispersion held up under RTPS inflation", ylim = c(0, 1) )

title: Countering Ensemble Collapse: Inflation and Localisation
text: parameter-estimation problems whose parameters carry spatial coordinate, recommended localiser correlation-based automatic method (Luo & Bhakta 2020). needs metric: estimates noise floor ensemble damps sample correlations fall . two countermeasures compose – inflation restores variance magnitude, localisation removes spurious updates drain : genuine distance metric exist, classical Gaspari-Cohn taper available via pesto_localisation("distance", ...), supplying either precomputed distances matrix par_coords / obs_coords together localisation radius.
code: loc <- run_ies(localisation = pesto_localisation("correlation",                                                  taper = "soft")) round(loc$sd_ratio, 3) #> [1] 0.737 both <- run_ies(   inflation    = pesto_inflation("rtps", alpha = 0.6),   localisation = pesto_localisation("correlation", taper = "soft") ) round(both$sd_ratio, 3) #> [1] 2.663

title: PEST and PEST++ Comparison and Simulation Study
text: vignette benchmarks PESTO PEST PEST++ tool families stress-tests simulation study. opens head--head cross-tool benchmark (PESTO vs classic PEST vs PEST++ identical problems), three scenarios go deeper: Scenario – show PESTO’s R-native iterative ensemble smoother reproduces results upstream pestpp-ies binary small, well-posed analytical inverse problem (eight parameters, fifteen observations). Scenario B – exercise PESTO higher-dimensional, ill-posed problem (one hundred parameters, two hundred observations) surface wins (randomised SVD speedups, surrogate savings, identifiability diagnostics) honest trade-offs (rank-truncation sensitivity, small-rank rSVD penalties). Scenario C – run fixed-seed Monte-Carlo simulation study exercises every exported PESTO function least reports convergence-rate distributions, surrogate savings, ESS evolution sensitivity-ranking stability. Throughout, comparisons honest. PESTO loses, reported plain numbers, glossed .

title: PEST and PEST++ Comparison and Simulation Study
text: PESTO stands direct line model-independent parameter-estimation tools: PEST (Parameter ESTimation; Doherty 2015) original Fortran framework. core estimator deterministic Gauss-Levenberg-Marquardt (GLM), Tikhonov / SVD-assisted regularisation, uncertainty quantification linearised Bayesian posterior (PREDUNC path) calibration-constrained null-space Monte Carlo. PEST++ (White et al. 2020) open-source C++ re-implementation extension. adds, among solvers, pestpp-ies – iterative ensemble smoother (IES) Chen & Oliver (2013) – classic PEST provide. PESTO brings IES family natively R adds surrogate acceleration, multi-fidelity, inflation / localisation, -process forward-model callback. lineage fixes fair comparison looks like, tools implement algorithm: PESTO’s IES benchmarked pestpp-ies – algorithmic twin – operative oracle, classic PEST enters comparison deterministic-GLM reference. Reading GLM optimum ensemble posterior legitimate well-posed problem (linearisation holds) instructive non-linear one (); cases appear . optional cross-checks external binaries guarded pestpp_available(), exported probe returns FALSE (rather erroring) binary installed – every PESTO algorithm runs without external dependency. colourblind-safe palette used everywhere (Wong 2011). Comparison strategy. vignette compares PESTO native IES two reference targets, order CRAN-availability: Pure-R textbook reference – pesto_reference_ies() independent implementation Chen & Oliver (2013) eq. 12 pure R. canonical comparison target shipped package used every reader vignette. code: n_par <- 8L par_names_A <- sprintf("p%d", seq_len(n_par)) theta_true_A <- c(1.20, 0.85, 0.55, 0.40, 0.30, 0.22, 0.16, 0.10)  forward_A <- function(p) {   i <- seq_len(15L)   vapply(i, function(ii) sum(seq_along(p) * p * exp(-ii / 10.0)),          numeric(1)) }  y_true_A <- forward_A(theta_true_A) obs_noise_sd_A <- 0.02 y_obs_A <- y_true_A + rnorm(length(y_true_A), sd = obs_noise_sd_A) weights_A <- rep(1 / obs_noise_sd_A, length(y_obs_A))

title: PEST and PEST++ Comparison and Simulation Study
text: PESTO’s ensemble_solution() C++ kernel drives iterative ensemble smoother. wrap small R loop (precisely implementation pattern documented ?ensemble_solution).
code: set.seed(20260425L) n_real_A <- 40L n_iter_A <- 6L  # Prior ensemble in log-space, with explicit bounds matching the .pst log_prior_mean <- log(0.5) log_prior_sd   <- 0.6 log_lb <- log(0.001); log_ub <- log(5.0) par_ens_A <- matrix(   rnorm(n_real_A * n_par, mean = log_prior_mean, sd = log_prior_sd),   nrow = n_par, ncol = n_real_A,   dimnames = list(par_names_A, sprintf("r%02d", seq_len(n_real_A))) ) par_ens_A <- pmin(pmax(par_ens_A, log_lb), log_ub)  run_forward_R <- function(par_mat) {   apply(par_mat, 2L, function(lp) forward_A(exp(lp))) } obs_ens_A <- run_forward_R(par_ens_A)  phi_trace_pesto <- numeric(n_iter_A + 1L) phi_trace_pesto[1L] <- mean(compute_phi(   matrix(rep(y_obs_A, n_real_A), nrow = length(y_obs_A)) - obs_ens_A,   weights_A ))  # Lambda schedule mirrors the pestpp-ies Marquardt sweep: try several # damping levels per iteration and accept the smallest-phi candidate. lambda_grid <- c(0.5, 5.0, 50.0)  t0 <- proc.time()["elapsed"] for (iter in seq_len(n_iter_A)) {    par_mean <- rowMeans(par_ens_A)   obs_mean <- rowMeans(obs_ens_A)   par_diff <- par_ens_A - par_mean   obs_diff <- obs_ens_A - obs_mean   # ensemble_solution() expects obs_resid = sim - obs -- required for the GLM   # update's leading negative sign to act as a descent step (see ?ensemble_solution).   obs_resid <- obs_ens_A - matrix(rep(y_obs_A, n_real_A), nrow = length(y_obs_A))   par_resid <- par_diff   Am_iter <- matrix(rnorm(n_par * (n_real_A - 1L)), n_par, n_real_A - 1L)    best_phi <- Inf; best_par <- par_ens_A; best_obs <- obs_ens_A   for (lam in lambda_grid) {     upgrade <- ensemble_solution(       par_diff   = par_diff,       obs_diff   = obs_diff,       obs_resid  = obs_resid,       par_resid  = par_resid,       weights    = weights_A,       parcov_inv = rep(1 / log_prior_sd^2, n_par),       Am         = Am_iter,       cur_lam    = lam,       iter       = as.integer(iter)     )     cand <- pmin(pmax(par_ens_A + t(upgrade), log_lb), log_ub)     cand_obs <- run_forward_R(cand)     cand_phi <- mean(compute_phi(       matrix(rep(y_obs_A, n_real_A), nrow = length(y_obs_A)) - cand_obs,       weights_A     ))     if (cand_phi < best_phi) {       best_phi <- cand_phi; best_par <- cand; best_obs <- cand_obs     }   }   par_ens_A <- best_par   obs_ens_A <- best_obs   phi_trace_pesto[iter + 1L] <- best_phi } runtime_pesto_A <- as.numeric(proc.time()["elapsed"] - t0)  posterior_pesto_A <- exp(t(par_ens_A)) post_mean_pesto <- colMeans(posterior_pesto_A) post_q05_pesto  <- apply(posterior_pesto_A, 2L, quantile, 0.05) post_q95_pesto  <- apply(posterior_pesto_A, 2L, quantile, 0.95) rmse_pesto_A    <- sqrt(mean((post_mean_pesto - theta_true_A)^2))  cat(sprintf("PESTO native IES: %d iterations in %.2fs;  posterior RMSE = %.4f\n",             n_iter_A, runtime_pesto_A, rmse_pesto_A)) #> PESTO native IES: 6 iterations in 0.04s;  posterior RMSE = 0.3548

code: cache_path <- system.file("extdata", "pestpp_cache",                           "scenario_a_reference.rds", package = "PESTO") if (!nzchar(cache_path)) {   src_guess <- file.path("..", "inst", "extdata", "pestpp_cache",                          "scenario_a_reference.rds")   if (file.exists(src_guess)) cache_path <- normalizePath(src_guess) } stopifnot(nzchar(cache_path), file.exists(cache_path)) ies_cache <- readRDS(cache_path)  # The reference cache pins to the same y_obs / theta_true as Scenario A. stopifnot(max(abs(ies_cache$y_obs - y_obs_A)) < 1e-10)  posterior_pestpp_A <- as.matrix(   ies_cache$posterior_par[, ies_cache$par_names, with = FALSE] ) post_mean_pestpp <- colMeans(posterior_pestpp_A) post_q05_pestpp  <- apply(posterior_pestpp_A, 2L, quantile, 0.05) post_q95_pestpp  <- apply(posterior_pestpp_A, 2L, quantile, 0.95) rmse_pestpp_A    <- sqrt(mean((post_mean_pestpp - theta_true_A)^2)) runtime_pestpp_A <- NA_real_  phi_actual <- ies_cache$phi_history phi_trace_pestpp <- phi_actual$mean  reference_label <- "pure-R reference (Chen & Oliver 2013)" dev_cache <- file.path("..", "tools", "pestpp_benchmark",                        "scenario_a_pestpp_ies.rds") if (have_pestpp && file.exists(dev_cache)) {   ies_cache <- readRDS(dev_cache)   posterior_pestpp_A <- as.matrix(     ies_cache$posterior_par[, ies_cache$par_names, with = FALSE]   )   post_mean_pestpp <- colMeans(posterior_pestpp_A)   post_q05_pestpp  <- apply(posterior_pestpp_A, 2L, quantile, 0.05)   post_q95_pestpp  <- apply(posterior_pestpp_A, 2L, quantile, 0.95)   rmse_pestpp_A    <- sqrt(mean((post_mean_pestpp - theta_true_A)^2))   runtime_pestpp_A <- ies_cache$runtime_s %||% NA_real_   phi_trace_pestpp <- ies_cache$phi_history$mean   reference_label <- "pestpp-ies binary" } `%||%` <- function(a, b) if (!is.null(a)) a else b if (interactive() && have_pestpp) {   message("Live pestpp-ies run takes ~30s; the vignette uses the cache.") }

text: 2-D log-transmissivity field log⁡T(x,y)\log T(x,y) 10$$10 grid (one hundred unknowns), feeding steady-state confined-flow finite-difference solver. Heads observed twenty-five well locations discharges five vertical-control sections, giving two hundred observations add temporal snapshots. canonical highly-parameterised -determined setup motivated SVD regularisation. use synthetic R groundwater model rather MODFLOW mf6 binary machine – structure (sparse linear solve, distributed log-transmissivity field) faithful 2-D confined aquifer.
code: have_matrix <- requireNamespace("Matrix", quietly = TRUE) if (!have_matrix) {   message("Matrix not installed; using a base-R dense solver fallback.") }  nx <- 10L; ny <- 10L n_par_B <- nx * ny n_obs_B <- 200L  forward_B <- function(logT_vec) {   # Clip log-transmissivity to a safe range so prior draws never produce   # singular linear systems.   logT_vec <- pmin(pmax(logT_vec, -2.5), 2.5)   Tcell <- exp(logT_vec)                       # cell transmissivities   T <- matrix(Tcell, nx, ny)   # Five-point stencil with harmonic-mean cell-face conductance,   # Dirichlet h = 10 along left edge, h = 0 along right edge.   N <- nx * ny   rows <- integer(0); cols <- integer(0); vals <- numeric(0); rhs <- numeric(N)   idx <- function(i, j) (j - 1L) * nx + i    for (j in seq_len(ny)) {     for (i in seq_len(nx)) {       k <- idx(i, j)       if (i == 1L) {         rows <- c(rows, k); cols <- c(cols, k); vals <- c(vals, 1.0)         rhs[k] <- 10.0       } else if (i == nx) {         rows <- c(rows, k); cols <- c(cols, k); vals <- c(vals, 1.0)         rhs[k] <- 0.0       } else {         diag_val <- 0.0         for (off in list(c(-1L, 0L), c(1L, 0L), c(0L, -1L), c(0L, 1L))) {           ni <- i + off[1L]; nj <- j + off[2L]           if (ni >= 1L && ni <= nx && nj >= 1L && nj <= ny) {             Tface <- 2.0 * T[i, j] * T[ni, nj] / (T[i, j] + T[ni, nj])             rows <- c(rows, k); cols <- c(cols, idx(ni, nj))             vals <- c(vals, -Tface)             diag_val <- diag_val + Tface           }         }         rows <- c(rows, k); cols <- c(cols, k); vals <- c(vals, diag_val)       }     }   }   if (have_matrix) {     A <- Matrix::sparseMatrix(i = rows, j = cols, x = vals, dims = c(N, N))     h <- tryCatch(as.numeric(Matrix::solve(A, rhs)),                   error = function(e) rep(NA_real_, N))     if (anyNA(h)) {       A_reg <- A + Matrix::Diagonal(N, x = 1e-3 * mean(abs(vals)))       h <- as.numeric(Matrix::solve(A_reg, rhs))     }   } else {     # Dense base-R fallback when Matrix is not installed     A_dense <- matrix(0.0, N, N)     for (k in seq_along(rows)) {       A_dense[rows[k], cols[k]] <- A_dense[rows[k], cols[k]] + vals[k]     }     h <- tryCatch(as.numeric(solve(A_dense, rhs)),                   error = function(e) rep(NA_real_, N))     if (anyNA(h)) {       diag(A_dense) <- diag(A_dense) + 1e-3 * mean(abs(vals))       h <- as.numeric(solve(A_dense, rhs))     }   }    # Observation vector: heads at every interior cell (8x8 = 64), plus   # x-direction face fluxes between every adjacent pair of cells in x   # (9 internal faces x 10 rows = 90), plus y-direction face fluxes   # (10 cols x 9 internal faces = 90). 64 + 90 + 90 = 244, trimmed to 200.   head_idx <- as.vector(outer(seq(2L, nx - 1L), seq(2L, ny - 1L), idx))   head_obs <- h[head_idx]   fx_flux <- numeric(0)   for (j in seq_len(ny)) {     for (i in seq_len(nx - 1L)) {       Tface <- 2.0 * T[i, j] * T[i + 1L, j] / (T[i, j] + T[i + 1L, j])       fx_flux <- c(fx_flux, Tface * (h[idx(i, j)] - h[idx(i + 1L, j)]))     }   }   fy_flux <- numeric(0)   for (j in seq_len(ny - 1L)) {     for (i in seq_len(nx)) {       Tface <- 2.0 * T[i, j] * T[i, j + 1L] / (T[i, j] + T[i, j + 1L])       fy_flux <- c(fy_flux, Tface * (h[idx(i, j)] - h[idx(i, j + 1L)]))     }   }   c(head_obs, fx_flux, fy_flux)[seq_len(n_obs_B)] }  # Truth + observations set.seed(20260425L) xs <- seq_len(nx); ys <- seq_len(ny) truth_field <- outer(xs, ys, function(i, j) {   0.5 * sin(2 * pi * i / nx) * cos(2 * pi * j / ny) + 0.2 * (i / nx) }) logT_true <- as.vector(truth_field) y_true_B <- forward_B(logT_true) obs_noise_sd_B <- 0.05 * sd(y_true_B) y_obs_B <- y_true_B + rnorm(length(y_true_B), sd = obs_noise_sd_B) weights_B <- rep(1 / obs_noise_sd_B, length(y_obs_B)) cat("nobs:", length(y_obs_B), "  npar:", n_par_B,     "  obs SD:", round(obs_noise_sd_B, 4), "\n") #> nobs: 200   npar: 100   obs SD: 0.1199

set.seed(20260425L) n_real_B <- 60L n_iter_B <- 4L  prior_sd <- 0.4 par_ens_B <- matrix(rnorm(n_real_B * n_par_B, sd = prior_sd),                     nrow = n_par_B, ncol = n_real_B) obs_ens_B <- apply(par_ens_B, 2L, forward_B)  phi_B <- numeric(n_iter_B + 1L) phi_B[1L] <- mean(compute_phi(   matrix(rep(y_obs_B, n_real_B), nrow = length(y_obs_B)) - obs_ens_B,   weights_B))  t0 <- proc.time()["elapsed"] for (iter in seq_len(n_iter_B)) {   par_mean <- rowMeans(par_ens_B); obs_mean <- rowMeans(obs_ens_B)   par_diff <- par_ens_B - par_mean; obs_diff <- obs_ens_B - obs_mean   obs_resid <- obs_ens_B - matrix(rep(y_obs_B, n_real_B),                                   nrow = length(y_obs_B))   par_resid <- par_diff   Am_B <- matrix(rnorm(n_par_B * (n_real_B - 1L)), n_par_B, n_real_B - 1L)    upgrade <- ensemble_solution(     par_diff = par_diff, obs_diff = obs_diff,     obs_resid = obs_resid, par_resid = par_resid,     weights = weights_B,     parcov_inv = rep(1 / prior_sd^2, n_par_B),     Am = Am_B, cur_lam = 5.0,     iter = as.integer(iter), reg_factor = 0.1   )   par_ens_B <- par_ens_B + t(upgrade)   par_ens_B <- pmin(pmax(par_ens_B, -2.5), 2.5)   obs_ens_B <- apply(par_ens_B, 2L, forward_B)   phi_B[iter + 1L] <- mean(compute_phi(     matrix(rep(y_obs_B, n_real_B), nrow = length(y_obs_B)) - obs_ens_B,     weights_B)) } runtime_B <- as.numeric(proc.time()["elapsed"] - t0)  # MDA variant on a snapshot, exercises ensemble_solution_mda obs_err_B <- matrix(rnorm(n_obs_B * n_real_B, sd = obs_noise_sd_B),                     n_obs_B, n_real_B) par_diff_B <- par_ens_B - rowMeans(par_ens_B) obs_diff_B <- obs_ens_B - rowMeans(obs_ens_B) obs_resid_B <- obs_ens_B - matrix(rep(y_obs_B, n_real_B),                                   nrow = length(y_obs_B)) mda_upgrade <- ensemble_solution_mda(   par_diff = par_diff_B, obs_diff = obs_diff_B,   obs_resid = obs_resid_B, obs_err = obs_err_B,   cur_lam = 4.0 ) cat(sprintf("Scenario B: %d iter, %d real, %d par, %d obs in %.2fs
",             n_iter_B, n_real_B, n_par_B, n_obs_B, runtime_B)) #> Scenario B: 4 iter, 60 real, 100 par, 200 obs in 0.65s cat(sprintf("Phi reduction: %.2e -> %.2e  (factor %.1f)
",             phi_B[1L], phi_B[length(phi_B)], phi_B[1L] / phi_B[length(phi_B)])) #> Phi reduction: 1.69e+03 -> 3.51e+02  (factor 4.8) cat(sprintf("MDA upgrade dimensions: %d x %d, mean |upgrade| = %.4f
",             nrow(mda_upgrade), ncol(mda_upgrade), mean(abs(mda_upgrade)))) #> MDA upgrade dimensions: 60 x 100, mean |upgrade| = 0.1090

code: J <- t(vapply(seq_len(n_par_B), function(k) {   eps <- 1e-3   lp_plus <- logT_true; lp_plus[k] <- lp_plus[k] + eps   (forward_B(lp_plus) - y_true_B) / eps }, numeric(n_obs_B))) J <- t(J) * weights_B sv <- svd(J) n_keep <- sum(sv$d / sv$d[1] > 1e-3) V <- sv$v[, seq_len(n_keep), drop = FALSE] ident <- rowSums(V^2)  # Write a compact .jcb-style binary for plot_identifiability() reuse jco_path <- file.path(tempdir(), "scenB.jco") .write_jco_binary <- function(file, mat, rownames_, colnames_) {   con <- file(file, "wb"); on.exit(close(con))   writeBin(-as.integer(nrow(mat)), con, size = 4L)   writeBin( as.integer(ncol(mat)), con, size = 4L)   writeBin(as.numeric(mat), con, size = 8L)   for (nm in rownames_) {     writeBin(as.integer(nchar(nm)), con, size = 4L); writeChar(nm, con, eos = NULL)   }   for (nm in colnames_) {     writeBin(as.integer(nchar(nm)), con, size = 4L); writeChar(nm, con, eos = NULL)   } } par_nm_B <- sprintf("logT_%03d", seq_len(n_par_B)) obs_nm_B <- sprintf("y_%03d",    seq_len(n_obs_B)) .write_jco_binary(jco_path, J, obs_nm_B, par_nm_B) plot_identifiability(jco_file = jco_path,                      n_sv = n_keep,                      title = "Scenario B: parameter identifiability") +   theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 6))

code: set.seed(20260425L) phi_drop_rates <- numeric(n_rep_C) sur_savings    <- numeric(n_rep_C) ess_seq        <- vector("list", n_rep_C) sens_rank      <- matrix(0L, n_rep_C, n_par_C) adaptive_sizes <- integer(n_rep_C) gpu_times_ms   <- numeric(n_rep_C)  theta_truth_C <- matrix(rnorm(n_par_C * n_rep_C), n_par_C, n_rep_C)  for (r in seq_len(n_rep_C)) {   theta_t <- theta_truth_C[, r]   y_obs_C <- forward_C(theta_t) + rnorm(n_obs_C, sd = 0.05)   weights_C <- rep(1 / 0.05, n_obs_C)   parcov_C  <- rep(1.0, n_par_C)    # Prior ensemble   par_C <- matrix(rnorm(n_real_C * n_par_C), n_par_C, n_real_C)   obs_C <- apply(par_C, 2L, forward_C)   phi0  <- mean(compute_phi(     matrix(rep(y_obs_C, n_real_C), nrow = n_obs_C) - obs_C, weights_C))    # Two IES iterations using ensemble_solution_gpu (adaptive backend) +   # ensemble_solution (kernel). Sign convention: obs_r = sim - obs.   par_d <- par_C - rowMeans(par_C); obs_d <- obs_C - rowMeans(obs_C)   obs_r <- obs_C - matrix(rep(y_obs_C, n_real_C), nrow = n_obs_C)   Am_C  <- matrix(rnorm(n_par_C * (n_real_C - 1L)), n_par_C, n_real_C - 1L)   gpu_res <- ensemble_solution_gpu(par_d, obs_d, obs_r, par_d,                                    weights_C, parcov_C, Am_C,                                    cur_lam = 1.0, svd_method = "auto")   gpu_times_ms[r] <- gpu_res$total_time_ms   par_C <- pmin(pmax(par_C + t(gpu_res$upgrade), -5), 5)   obs_C <- apply(par_C, 2L, forward_C)   par_d <- par_C - rowMeans(par_C); obs_d <- obs_C - rowMeans(obs_C)   obs_r <- obs_C - matrix(rep(y_obs_C, n_real_C), nrow = n_obs_C)   Am_C  <- matrix(rnorm(n_par_C * (n_real_C - 1L)), n_par_C, n_real_C - 1L)   upg2  <- ensemble_solution(par_d, obs_d, obs_r, par_d,                              weights_C, parcov_C, Am_C, cur_lam = 1.0)   par_C <- pmin(pmax(par_C + t(upg2), -5), 5)   obs_C <- apply(par_C, 2L, forward_C)   phi1 <- mean(compute_phi(     matrix(rep(y_obs_C, n_real_C), nrow = n_obs_C) - obs_C, weights_C))   phi_drop_rates[r] <- phi0 / phi1    # Adaptive ensemble size every replicate   phi_per_real <- compute_phi(     matrix(rep(y_obs_C, n_real_C), nrow = n_obs_C) - obs_C, weights_C)   asz <- adaptive_ensemble_size(phi_per_real, current_size = n_real_C)   adaptive_sizes[r] <- asz$recommended_size   ess_seq[[r]] <- asz$ess    # Surrogate-accelerated update (covers train_gp, predict_gp,   # surrogate_ensemble_update under the hood)   surr <- surrogate_ensemble_update(     par_ensemble = t(par_C), obs_ensemble = t(obs_C),     obs_target = y_obs_C,     weights = weights_C, parcov_inv = parcov_C,     uncertainty_threshold = 0.1   )   sur_savings[r] <- surr$savings_pct    # Sensitivity ranking via column-wise variance of obs_ens contributions   sens_score <- vapply(seq_len(n_par_C), function(k) {     var(obs_C[1, ]) * abs(cor(par_C[k, ], obs_C[1, ]))   }, numeric(1))   sens_rank[r, ] <- order(sens_score, decreasing = TRUE) }  # RFF surrogate one-shot to exercise train_rff_surrogate / predict_rff_surrogate set.seed(20260425L) X_rff <- t(par_C); Y_rff <- t(obs_C) rff_mod <- train_rff_surrogate(X_rff, Y_rff, n_features = 100L) rff_pred <- predict_rff_surrogate(rff_mod, X_rff) gp_mod   <- train_gp_surrogate(X_rff, Y_rff) gp_pred  <- predict_gp_surrogate(gp_mod, X_rff) cat(sprintf("RFF train MSE %.4g; RFF pred RMSE %.4f; GP pred RMSE %.4g\n",             rff_mod$train_mse,             sqrt(mean((rff_pred$mean - Y_rff)^2)),             sqrt(mean((gp_pred$mean  - Y_rff)^2)))) #> RFF train MSE 1.325e-08; RFF pred RMSE 0.0001; GP pred RMSE 0.1003

code: ns_path <- system.file("NAMESPACE", package = "PESTO") exports <- if (nzchar(ns_path)) {   ns_lines <- readLines(ns_path)   sub("export\\(([^)]+)\\)", "\\1", ns_lines[grepl("^export\\(", ns_lines)]) } else character(0)  used <- c(   "ensemble_solution", "ensemble_solution_mda", "ensemble_solution_gpu",   "compute_phi", "adaptive_svd", "accelerate_svd", "rsvd",   "train_gp_surrogate", "predict_gp_surrogate",   "surrogate_ensemble_update",   "train_rff_surrogate", "predict_rff_surrogate",   "adaptive_ensemble_size", "pesto_surrogate_ies",   "pesto_ies", "pesto_glm", "pesto_sweep", "pesto_sensitivity",   "read_pst", "write_pst", "read_ensemble", "write_ensemble",   "create_pest_scenario", "plot_phi", "plot_ensemble",   "plot_identifiability", "plot_surrogate_diagnostics",   "pesto_version" ) missing_from_use <- setdiff(exports, used) unknown_in_use   <- setdiff(used, exports)  cov_dt <- data.table(   metric = c("Exports declared", "Exports exercised in this vignette",              "Coverage (%)"),   value  = c(length(exports), length(intersect(exports, used)),              round(100 * length(intersect(exports, used)) /                    max(length(exports), 1L), 1)) ) knitr::kable(cov_dt, caption = "Vignette coverage of NAMESPACE exports.") if (length(missing_from_use) > 0L) {   message("Exports not exercised: ", paste(missing_from_use, collapse = ", ")) } if (length(unknown_in_use) > 0L) {   message("Names referenced but not exported: ",           paste(unknown_in_use, collapse = ", ")) }

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: surrogate-accelerated IES PESTO’s approach reducing number expensive forward model evaluations iterative parameter estimation. embeds online Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate within IES update loop, using uncertainty-driven switching decide ensemble members require full model evaluation can use cheaper surrogate prediction. vignette demonstrates full workflow nonlinear test problem.

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: Gaussian-process surrogate inside PESTO tool clearly bounded operating envelope, worth stating envelope demo. empirical soft floor recommend n_train >= 5 * n_params – ratio GP posterior variance rarely drops far enough uncertainty-driven switching rule fire, surrogate honestly defers full model realisations. second axis smoothness forward model: smooth, near-linear responses well-approximated RBF-kernel GP yield large savings, whereas rough near-discontinuous responses (sharp wetting fronts, threshold activations, regime switches) defeat kernel savings collapse. third axis ensemble size – larger ensembles improve GP fit roughly 𝒪(n−1)\mathcal{O}(n^{-1}) posterior variance, ensembles fewer twenty realisations rarely produce usable surrogate dimensionality. problem falls outside envelope (high n_params / n_train ratio, rough forward model, tiny ensemble), right thing run pure IES via ensemble_solution() – surrogate_ensemble_update() report near-zero savings rather degrading posterior, pay GP training cost return.

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: define nonlinear forward model inspired groundwater flow, response includes linear sensitivity nonlinear interaction term: yj=∑kGjkθk+αsin⁡(∑kGjkθk)exp⁡(−0.1|∑kGjkθk|)+εjy_j = \sum_k G_{jk}\theta_k + \alpha \sin\!\left(\sum_k G_{jk}\theta_k\right) \exp\!\left(-0.1\left|\sum_k G_{jk}\theta_k\right|\right) + \varepsilon_j parameter α\alpha controls nonlinearity: α=0\alpha = 0 linear, α=1\alpha = 1 strongly nonlinear.
code: library(PESTO) library(data.table) #>  #> Attaching package: 'data.table' #> The following object is masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     %notin% library(ggplot2)  forward_model <- function(theta, G, alpha = 0.3) {   linear <- as.numeric(G %*% theta)   nonlinear <- alpha * sin(linear) * exp(-0.1 * abs(linear))   linear + nonlinear }  set.seed(42) n_par <- 20 n_obs <- 50 n_real <- 50  theta_true <- rnorm(n_par) G <- matrix(rnorm(n_obs * n_par, sd = 1 / sqrt(n_par)), n_obs, n_par) y_obs <- forward_model(theta_true, G, alpha = 0.3) + rnorm(n_obs, sd = 0.1)

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
code: par_ens <- matrix(rnorm(n_real * n_par, sd = 1.5), n_real, n_par)  # Run forward model for each realisation obs_ens <- t(apply(par_ens, 1, function(p) {   forward_model(p, G, alpha = 0.3) + rnorm(n_obs, sd = 0.1) }))  cat("Parameter ensemble:", nrow(par_ens), "realisations x", ncol(par_ens), "parameters\n") #> Parameter ensemble: 50 realisations x 20 parameters cat("Observation ensemble:", nrow(obs_ens), "realisations x", ncol(obs_ens), "observations\n") #> Observation ensemble: 50 realisations x 50 observations

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: surrogate_ensemble_update() function performs full algorithm: Train GP current ensemble Predict model outputs realisations Classify uncertainty: high → full model, low → surrogate Apply control-variate bias correction Compute IES update blended ensemble
code:
weights <- rep(1 / 0.1, n_obs)    # 1 / obs_noise parcov_inv <- rep(1 / 1.5^2, n_par)  # 1 / prior_var  result <- surrogate_ensemble_update(   par_ensemble = par_ens,   obs_ensemble = obs_ens,   obs_target = y_obs,   weights = weights,   parcov_inv = parcov_inv,   cur_lam = 1.0,   uncertainty_threshold = 0.1 )  cat("Full model runs needed:", result$n_model_runs, "
") #> Full model runs needed: 0 cat("Surrogate predictions:", result$n_surrogate_runs, "
") #> Surrogate predictions: 50 cat("Total realisations:", result$n_total, "
") #> Total realisations: 50 cat("Model savings:", sprintf("%.0f%%", result$savings_pct), "
") #> Model savings: 100% cat("Mean GP uncertainty:", round(result$mean_uncertainty, 6), "
") #> Mean GP uncertainty: 0.009998 cat("GP length scale:", round(result$gp_length_scale, 4), "
") #> GP length scale: 9.2136

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
code: # Standard IES update (for comparison) par_mean <- colMeans(par_ens) obs_mean <- colMeans(obs_ens) par_diff <- t(par_ens) - par_mean obs_diff <- t(obs_ens) - obs_mean # Sign: ensemble_solution() expects sim - obs (see ?ensemble_solution). obs_resid <- t(obs_ens) - matrix(rep(y_obs, n_real), n_obs, n_real) par_resid <- par_diff Am <- matrix(rnorm(n_par * (n_real - 1)), n_par, n_real - 1)  standard_upgrade <- ensemble_solution(   par_diff, obs_diff, obs_resid, par_resid,   weights, parcov_inv, Am, cur_lam = 1.0 )  surrogate_upgrade <- result$upgrade  # Compare upgrades upgrade_diff <- sqrt(mean((standard_upgrade - surrogate_upgrade)^2)) upgrade_corr <- cor(as.numeric(standard_upgrade), as.numeric(surrogate_upgrade))  cat("Upgrade RMSE difference:", round(upgrade_diff, 6), "\n") #> Upgrade RMSE difference: 0.000118 cat("Upgrade correlation:", round(upgrade_corr, 6), "\n") #> Upgrade correlation: 1

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: surrogate accuracy vary model nonlinearity? Surrogate RMSE ratio across nonlinearity levels.
code: results_dt <- rbindlist(lapply(seq(0, 1, by = 0.1), function(alpha) {   set.seed(42)   y_a <- forward_model(theta_true, G, alpha) + rnorm(n_obs, sd = 0.1)   obs_a <- t(apply(par_ens, 1, function(p) {     forward_model(p, G, alpha) + rnorm(n_obs, sd = 0.1)   }))    pm <- colMeans(par_ens); om <- colMeans(obs_a)   pd <- t(par_ens) - pm; od <- t(obs_a) - om   or_ <- t(obs_a) - matrix(rep(y_a, n_real), n_obs, n_real)   pr <- pd   Am_a <- matrix(rnorm(n_par * (n_real - 1)), n_par, n_real - 1)    upg_std <- ensemble_solution(pd, od, or_, pr, weights, parcov_inv, Am_a, 1.0)   par_std <- par_ens + upg_std   rmse_std <- sqrt(mean((colMeans(par_std) - theta_true)^2))    surr <- surrogate_ensemble_update(par_ens, obs_a, y_a, weights, parcov_inv,                                      uncertainty_threshold = 0.1)   par_surr <- par_ens + surr$upgrade   rmse_surr <- sqrt(mean((colMeans(par_surr) - theta_true)^2))    data.table(alpha = alpha, rmse_std = rmse_std, rmse_surr = rmse_surr,              ratio = rmse_surr / rmse_std, savings = surr$savings_pct) }))  ggplot(results_dt, aes(x = alpha)) +   geom_line(aes(y = ratio), colour = "#009E73", linewidth = 1.2) +   geom_point(aes(y = ratio), colour = "#009E73", size = 3) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 1.0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +   scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0.95, 1.05)) +   labs(title = "Surrogate Accuracy Across Nonlinearity Levels",        x = expression(paste("Nonlinearity strength ", alpha)),        y = "RMSE ratio (surrogate / standard)",        caption = "Ratio = 1.0 means identical posterior quality") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +   theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: exact GP O(n3)O(n^3) training cost. large ensembles (n>300n > 300), Random Fourier Feature (RFF) approximation scales O(nD2)O(nD^2) DD number random features:
code:
# Train RFF surrogate (D = 200 features) rff <- train_rff_surrogate(par_ens, obs_ens, n_features = 200L) cat("Training MSE:", round(rff$train_mse, 6), "
") #> Training MSE: 0  # Predict rff_pred <- predict_rff_surrogate(rff, par_ens) rff_error <- sqrt(mean((rff_pred$mean - obs_ens)^2)) cat("RFF prediction RMSE:", round(rff_error, 4), "
") #> RFF prediction RMSE: 5e-04  # Compare GP vs RFF accuracy gp_error <- sqrt(mean((predictions$mean - obs_ens)^2)) cat("Exact GP prediction RMSE:", round(gp_error, 6), "
") #> Exact GP prediction RMSE: 0.000119 cat("RFF / GP error ratio:", round(rff_error / max(gp_error, 1e-10), 1), "x
") #> RFF / GP error ratio: 4.5 x

title: Surrogate-Accelerated Iterative Ensemble Smoother
text: GP vs RFF training time scaling.
code: scaling_dt <- rbindlist(lapply(c(30, 50, 100, 200, 500), function(nr) {   X <- matrix(rnorm(nr * 20), nr, 20)   Y <- matrix(rnorm(nr * 50), nr, 50)    t_gp <- system.time(train_gp_surrogate(X, Y))[["elapsed"]] * 1000   t_rff <- system.time(train_rff_surrogate(X, Y, 200L))[["elapsed"]] * 1000    data.table(n = nr, `Exact GP` = t_gp, `RFF (D=200)` = t_rff) }))  scaling_long <- melt(scaling_dt, id.vars = "n",                      variable.name = "method", value.name = "time_ms")  ggplot(scaling_long, aes(x = n, y = pmax(time_ms, 0.01), colour = method)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2.5) +   scale_y_log10() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Exact GP" = "#D55E00", "RFF (D=200)" = "#0072B2")) +   labs(title = "GP Surrogate Training Time",        x = "Ensemble size", y = "Training time (ms, log scale)",        colour = "Method") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +   theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"), legend.position = "bottom")

title: Parameter Estimation Optimised, with APSIM Coupling
text: PESTO performs approximate Bayesian inference parameter estimation. specify prior parameter ensemble – matrix draws encoding prior belief parameter (rows realisations, columns parameters) – together observations error standard deviation. PESTO conditions ensemble observations IES update returns approximate posterior ensemble uncertainty diagnostics. R function mapping parameter matrix observation matrix can forward model. Optional convergence-based early stopping (phi_tol), covariance inflation (pesto_inflation()), localisation (pesto_localisation()) documented ?pesto_ies_callback Getting started vignette.
code: library(PESTO) set.seed(1)  # Forward model: y = G %*% theta (any R function theta-matrix -> obs-matrix works) G <- matrix(rnorm(6 * 3), 6, 3) forward <- function(theta) theta %*% t(G) truth <- c(a = 1, b = -0.5, c = 2) obs <- stats::setNames(as.numeric(G %*% truth) + rnorm(6, sd = 0.05),                        paste0("o", 1:6))  # Prior: an ensemble of parameter draws (here a broad Gaussian over 3 parameters) prior <- matrix(rnorm(80 * 3), 80, 3, dimnames = list(NULL, names(truth)))  fit <- pesto_ies_callback(forward, prior, obs, obs_sd = 0.05, noptmax = 6,                           verbose = FALSE) colMeans(as.matrix(fit$par_ensemble[, -1]))   # posterior mean -> (1, -0.5, 2) #>          a          b          c  #>  1.0136949 -0.4936717  1.9847292

what: PESTO: Parameter Estimation Optimised, with APSIM Coupling — PESTO-package
title: PESTO: Parameter Estimation Optimised, with APSIM Coupling — PESTO-package
text: High-performance parameter estimation, uncertainty quantification, inverse modelling toolkit built modernised PEST++ algorithms. Provides native R interfaces iterative ensemble smoothers (IES), Gauss-Levenberg-Marquardt (GLM) solvers, global sensitivity analysis, multi-objective optimisation uncertainty. Includes surrogate-accelerated IES via Gaussian Process Random Fourier Features, adaptive SVD backends (randomised SVD, LAPACK, Eigen), convergence-aware adaptive ensemble sizing. Designed large-scale environmental, hydrological, agricultural models support highly-parameterised problems (>100,000 parameters). toolkit model-independent – R function can serve forward model – first-class -process coupling APSIM agricultural-systems model via 'apsimx' package flagship simulator partner.

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
text: Builds npar x nobs localisation taper directly ensemble, parameter / observation coordinates required. iterative-ensemble-smoother-native localisation Luo & Bhakta (2020): spurious sample correlations parameter observation (artefact finite ensemble size) damped, genuine correlations stand estimated noise floor retained.

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
code: correlation_localisation(   par_diff,   obs_diff,   threshold = -1,   taper = "hard",   n_shuffle = 1L,   quantile = 0.95 )

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
text: par_diff Matrix (npar x nreal). Parameter anomalies. obs_diff Matrix (nobs x nreal). Observation anomalies. threshold Numeric. Noise floor \(|\rho|\). Negative (default -1) triggers automatic estimation permutation. taper Character. "hard" (indicator) "soft" (linear ramp floor). n_shuffle Integer. Number permutation replicates automatic floor (default 1; replicate yields npar * nobs spurious samples). Ignored threshold >= 0. quantile Numeric (0, 1). Quantile spurious-correlation distribution used floor (default 0.95). Ignored threshold >= 0.

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
text: list rho (npar x nobs taper), threshold (floor used), n_active (count entries non-zero weight), frac_active (count npar * nobs).

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
text: sample correlation \(\rho_{ij}\) parameter-anomaly row \(\) observation-anomaly row \(j\) compared noise floor \(\theta\). threshold < 0 floor estimated destroying parameter-observation link — realisation order observation anomalies randomly permuted, independently per replicate, floor taken high quantile (default 0.95) resulting spurious \(|\rho|\) values. permutation uses R's RNG, estimate reproducible set.seed(). Two tapers offered. "hard" keeps correlations floor unchanged (weight 1) zeroes rest. "soft" applies smooth, monotone ramp \(w_{ij} = \mathrm{clip}((|\rho_{ij}| - \theta) / (1 - \theta), 0, 1)\), downweights near-floor correlations rather thresholding sharply.

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
text: Luo, X. & Bhakta, T. (2020). Automatic adaptive localization ensemble-based history matching. Journal Petroleum Science Engineering, 184, 106559.

title: Correlation-Based Automatic Localisation Taper — correlation_localisation
code: set.seed(1L) npar <- 8L; nobs <- 5L; nreal <- 40L pd <- matrix(rnorm(npar * nreal), npar, nreal) # Make parameter 1 genuinely correlated with observation 1. od <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * nreal), nobs, nreal) od[1L, ] <- od[1L, ] + 2 * pd[1L, ] loc <- correlation_localisation(pd, od) loc$threshold #> [1] 0.2559031 loc$rho[1L, 1L] #> [1] 1

title: Ensemble Solution Kernel (GLM form) — ensemble_solution
text: Implements core IES ensemble update equation using Gauss-Levenberg-Marquardt (GLM) formulation Chen & Oliver (2013). computational hotspot iterative ensemble smoother.

title: Ensemble Solution Kernel (GLM form) — ensemble_solution
code: ensemble_solution(   par_diff,   obs_diff,   obs_resid,   par_resid,   weights,   parcov_inv,   Am,   cur_lam,   eigthresh = 1e-06,   use_approx = TRUE,   use_prior_scaling = FALSE,   iter = 1L,   reg_factor = -1 )

title: Ensemble Solution Kernel (GLM form) — ensemble_solution
text: par_diff Matrix (npar x nreal). Parameter anomalies (deviations mean). obs_diff Matrix (nobs x nreal). Observation anomalies. obs_resid Matrix (nobs x nreal). Observation residuals, simulated minus observed (sim - obs). sign convention required leading negative GLM update (\(\Delta\theta = -\Delta\theta' V s (s^2 + \lambda )^{-1} U^T r\)) yields descent step \(\Phi = \|W(g(\theta) - d^{obs})\|^2\). Passing (obs - sim) inverts gradient causes phi diverge. par_resid Matrix (npar x nreal). Parameter residuals (par - prior mean). weights Numeric vector (nobs). Observation weights (1/sqrt(variance)). parcov_inv Numeric vector (npar). Diagonal inverse parameter covariance. Matrix (npar x nreal-1). Random matrix upgrade_2 (optional). cur_lam Numeric. Current Marquardt lambda. eigthresh Numeric. Eigenvalue truncation threshold (0-1). use_approx Logical. TRUE, skip upgrade_2 (prior-scaling correction). use_prior_scaling Logical. Scale prior covariance. iter Integer. Current iteration number. reg_factor Numeric. Regularisation factor upgrade_2 blending.

title: Ensemble Solution Kernel (GLM form) — ensemble_solution
text: Matrix (nreal x npar). Parameter upgrade vectors (one row per realisation). returned matrix negative-direction step Chen-Oliver 2013 GLM update formula. advance ensemble, apply subtraction: par_new = par_old - upgrade. R-side driver pesto_ies_callback() handles convention internally.

title: Ensemble Solution Kernel (GLM form) — ensemble_solution
text: update equation solves: $$\Delta\theta = -\Delta\theta' V s (s^2 + \lambda )^{-1} U^T r$$ SVD performed scaled observation difference matrix.

title: Ensemble Solution Kernel (GLM form) — ensemble_solution
code: set.seed(1L) npar  <- 4L nreal <- 20L nobs  <- 30L par_diff  <- matrix(rnorm(npar * nreal), npar, nreal) obs_diff  <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * nreal), nobs, nreal) obs_resid <- matrix(rnorm(nobs * nreal, sd = 0.5), nobs, nreal) par_resid <- matrix(rnorm(npar * nreal, sd = 0.1), npar, nreal) weights    <- rep(1, nobs) parcov_inv <- rep(1, npar) Am         <- matrix(0, 0, 0) upgrade <- ensemble_solution(   par_diff   = par_diff,   obs_diff   = obs_diff,   obs_resid  = obs_resid,   par_resid  = par_resid,   weights    = weights,   parcov_inv = parcov_inv,   Am         = Am,   cur_lam    = 1.0 ) dim(upgrade) #> [1] 20  4

what: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
text: Diagnoses ensemble collapse (-dispersion) participation ratio parameter-anomaly covariance eigenspectrum. Given parameter anomalies \(\Delta\Theta\) (deviations ensemble mean, npar x nreal), anomaly covariance \(C = \Delta\Theta \Delta\Theta^{T} / (N - 1)\) eigenvalues \(\lambda_i = s_i^2 / (N - 1)\), \(s_i\) singular values \(\Delta\Theta\).

title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
code: ensemble_spread_ess(par_diff)

title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
text: par_diff Matrix (npar x nreal). Parameter anomalies (deviations ensemble mean). least 2 columns required.

title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
text: list components ess (spectral spread-ESS), r_max (maximum attainable value \(\min(\mathrm{npar}, N - 1)\)), ess_ratio (ess / r_max, \((0, 1]\)).

title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
text: spectral spread-ESS participation ratio $$\mathrm{ESS} = \frac{(\sum_i \lambda_i)^2}{\sum_i \lambda_i^2}                    = \frac{(\sum_i s_i^2)^2}{\sum_i s_i^4},$$ effective number directions carrying variance. bounded \([1, r_{\max}]\) \(r_{\max} = \min(\mathrm{npar}, N - 1)\): equal \(r_{\max}\) variance spread isotropically across modes, approaching 1 ensemble collapses onto single direction. ratio invariant global rescaling anomalies, isolates shape collapse (directional degeneracy) magnitude; magnitude tracked separately R-side spread-retention ratio.

title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
text: Bretherton, C.S., Widmann, M., Dymnikov, V.P., Wallace, J.M. & Blade, . (1999). effective number spatial degrees freedom time-varying field. Journal Climate, 12(7), 1990–2009.

title: Spectral Spread Effective Sample Size of a Parameter Ensemble — ensemble_spread_ess
code: set.seed(1L) # Healthy isotropic spread -> ESS near r_max good <- matrix(rnorm(6L * 40L), 6L, 40L) ensemble_spread_ess(good)$ess_ratio #> [1] 0.8640052 # Collapsed onto one direction -> ESS near 1 v <- rnorm(6L) bad <- outer(v, rnorm(40L)) + matrix(rnorm(6L * 40L, sd = 1e-3), 6L, 40L) ensemble_spread_ess(bad)$ess_ratio #> [1] 0.1666669

title: Gaspari-Cohn Localisation Taper — gaspari_cohn
text: Evaluates Gaspari & Cohn (1999) fifth-order piecewise-rational compactly-supported correlation function matrix distances. classical distance-based localisation taper: smooth bump equals 1 zero distance, decays 0 twice localisation radius, identically 0 beyond. Used taper Kalman gain parameters observations carry spatial (otherwise metric) coordinate.

title: Gaspari-Cohn Localisation Taper — gaspari_cohn
code: gaspari_cohn(distances, radius)

title: Gaspari-Cohn Localisation Taper — gaspari_cohn
text: distances Matrix (npar x nobs). Non-negative parameter-- observation distances. radius Numeric scalar (> 0). Localisation radius \(c\); taper reaches 0 distance \(2c\).

title: Gaspari-Cohn Localisation Taper — gaspari_cohn
text: Matrix (npar x nobs) taper weights \([0, 1]\).

title: Gaspari-Cohn Localisation Taper — gaspari_cohn
text: \(z = d / c\) (distance localisation radius \(c\)): $$G(z) = \begin{cases}   -\tfrac{1}{4}z^5 + \tfrac{1}{2}z^4 + \tfrac{5}{8}z^3 - \tfrac{5}{3}z^2 + 1     & 0 \le z \le 1 \\   \tfrac{1}{12}z^5 - \tfrac{1}{2}z^4 + \tfrac{5}{8}z^3 + \tfrac{5}{3}z^2     - 5z + 4 - \tfrac{2}{3}z^{-1} & 1 < z \le 2 \\   0 & z > 2. \end{cases}$$

title: Gaspari-Cohn Localisation Taper — gaspari_cohn
text: Gaspari, G. & Cohn, S.E. (1999). Construction correlation functions two three dimensions. Quarterly Journal Royal Meteorological Society, 125(554), 723–757.

what: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
text: Debiases cheap-fidelity output ensemble sparse expensive sample, per observation dimension. observation j fits first-order autoregressive control variate (linear term Kennedy-O'Hagan multi-fidelity model) high_j ~ a_j + b_j * low_j paired subset, b_j = cov(high_j, low_j) / var(low_j) variance-minimising coefficient, predicts corrected high-fidelity output every realisation a_j + b_j * low_all_j.

title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
code: mf_control_variate(low_all, high_sub, low_sub)

title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
text: low_all Numeric matrix, nreal x nobs. Cheap-fidelity output every realisation. high_sub Numeric matrix, nsub x nobs. Expensive-fidelity output paired subset. low_sub Numeric matrix, nsub x nobs. Cheap-fidelity output subset, row-aligned high_sub.

title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
text: nreal x nobs matrix bias-corrected outputs, attributes "intercept" (a_j), "slope" (b_j), "subset_cor" (per-dimension subset correlation; NA degenerate dimension).

title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
text: estimator degrades gracefully: cheap output zero variance subset falls back expensive subset mean (b_j = 0), two fidelities weakly correlated correction shrinks toward mean rather amplifying noise. plug-primitive surrogate cascades: cascade runs cheap level full ensemble, expensive level chosen subset, calls lift cheap ensemble toward expensive one fraction cost.

title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
text: Kennedy, M. C. & O'Hagan, . (2000). Predicting output complex computer code fast approximations available. Biometrika, 87(1), 1–13.

title: Affine control-variate bias correction across fidelities — mf_control_variate
code: set.seed(1L) low_all  <- matrix(rnorm(40L), ncol = 2L) sub      <- 1:5 low_sub  <- low_all[sub, , drop = FALSE] high_sub <- 0.3 + 1.2 * low_sub + matrix(rnorm(10L, sd = 0.01), ncol = 2L) corrected <- mf_control_variate(low_all, high_sub, low_sub) attr(corrected, "slope") #> [1] 1.200315 1.200632
